characters.name: Nick Carraway
characters.description: The narrator, a young Midwesterner who has come East to learn the bond business. He is Daisy's second cousin once removed and serves as the story's observer. Works at Probity Trust and considers himself one of the few honest people he has ever known.

characters.name: Jay Gatsby
characters.description: Nick's mysterious neighbor who owns a colossal mansion and throws elaborate parties. Served in the First Division during the war. Claims to be an Oxford man, though Jordan doubts this. He possesses an extraordinary gift for hope and romantic readiness, and has a rare, reassuring smile.

physical.age: a year or two over thirty
physical.appearance: tanned skin drawn attractively tight on his face, short hair trimmed daily, elegant young roughneck

characters.name: Tom Buchanan
characters.description: Daisy's husband, a wealthy and physically powerful man from Nick's college days. He was a famous football player at Yale and comes from enormous wealth.

physical.appearance: sturdy straw-haired man with a hard mouth, arrogant eyes, and an enormously powerful body

characters.name: Daisy Buchanan
characters.description: Nick's second cousin, a beautiful and charming young woman married to Tom. She has a distinctive voice and manner that captivates men, though she appears cynical and troubled.

physical.appearance: sad and lovely face with bright eyes and a bright passionate mouth

characters.name: Jordan Baker
characters.description: A professional golfer and Daisy's friend, known for her athletic career and cool, skeptical demeanor. She will be playing in a tournament at Westchester. Has a history of dishonesty, including an incident where she allegedly moved her ball during a tournament.

physical.appearance: slender, small-breasted girl with erect carriage, grey sun-strained eyes, and autumn-leaf yellow hair

characters.name: Nick's father
characters.description: Nick's father who gave him advice about not criticizing others and who runs the family hardware business.

characters.name: Demaine
characters.description: The previous owner of Tom's mansion, described as an oil man.

characters.name: Goddard
characters.description: Author of 'The Rise of the Coloured Empires,' a book Tom references to support his racist views.

characters.name: Myrtle Wilson
characters.description: Tom Buchanan's mistress, wife of garage owner George Wilson. A sensual woman in her mid-thirties who transforms her personality depending on her surroundings.

physical.appearance: faintly stout but carries her flesh sensuously, thickish figure, no beauty but immediately perceptible vitality

characters.name: George Wilson
characters.description: Owner of a garage in the valley of ashes, married to Myrtle Wilson. A spiritless, struggling businessman unaware of his wife's affair.

physical.appearance: blond, spiritless man, anaemic, and faintly handsome with light blue eyes

characters.name: Catherine
characters.description: Myrtle Wilson's sister, a worldly woman of about thirty who lives at a hotel with a female friend.

physical.appearance: slender with solid, sticky bob of red hair, complexion powdered milky white, plucked and redrawn eyebrows, wears pottery bracelets

characters.name: Chester McKee
characters.description: A photographer who lives in the apartment below Tom and Myrtle's, described as pale and feminine. He works in the 'artistic game' and has photographed his wife many times.

characters.name: Lucille McKee
characters.description: Chester McKee's wife, who has been photographed by her husband 127 times since their marriage.

characters.name: Doctor T. J. Eckleburg
characters.description: An oculist who created the famous billboard advertisement with giant eyes overlooking the valley of ashes, then apparently moved away or died.

characters.name: Kaiser Wilhelm
characters.description: German Kaiser who is allegedly related to Jay Gatsby according to Catherine's gossip.

characters.name: Mrs. Eberhardt
characters.description: A woman who examines people's feet in their homes for medical purposes.

characters.name: Owl Eyes
characters.description: A stout, middle-aged man with enormous owl-eyed spectacles who is found in Gatsby's library examining the books. He is amazed to find they are real. Later crashes a car leaving the party.

characters.name: Mrs. Claud Roosevelt
characters.description: A woman who brought Owl Eyes to Gatsby's party.

characters.name: Lucille
characters.description: A party guest who attends Gatsby's parties regularly. She once tore her gown and Gatsby replaced it with a new one worth $265.

characters.name: Vladmir Tostoff
characters.description: A composer whose 'Jazz History of the World' was performed at Carnegie Hall and at Gatsby's party.

characters.name: Mrs. Sigourney Howard
characters.description: Jordan Baker's aunt, under whose name Jordan can be reached in the phone book.

characters.name: girl in Jersey City
characters.description: A girl who worked in the accounting department and had a brief affair with Nick. Her protective brother ended the relationship.

characters.name: girl back home
characters.description: A girl from Nick's hometown with whom he had an understanding. Nick wrote her letters signed 'Love, Nick' but planned to break it off. She developed a moustache of perspiration when playing tennis.

physical.appearance: faint moustache of perspiration on upper lip when playing tennis

timeline.event: Nick's father gives him advice about not criticizing others
Nick graduates from New Haven
Nick participates in World War I
Nick comes East to learn the bond business
Nick rents a house in West Egg
Nick drives to East Egg for dinner at the Buchanans
Tom receives phone calls during dinner from his mistress
Nick sees Gatsby reaching toward the green light
Myrtle and Tom first meet on a train to New York
Catherine visits Monte Carlo with a friend
Catherine attends a party at Gatsby's house
Tom and Nick take the train to New York and stop at Wilson's garage
timeline.date_in_story: a few days before the Fourth of July

timeline.event: Tom, Nick, and Myrtle go to New York and buy a dog
Party at Tom and Myrtle's apartment with the McKees and Catherine
Tom breaks Myrtle's nose during an argument about Daisy
Nick leaves the apartment with Mr. McKee
Nick waits for the 4 o'clock train at Pennsylvania Station
Jordan Baker loses in golf tournament finals
Gatsby sends Nick a formal invitation to his party
Nick attends Gatsby's party and meets his host
Jordan has private conversation with Gatsby
Owl Eyes crashes car leaving Gatsby's party
Nick begins working at Probity Trust
Nick has brief affair with girl from Jersey City
Nick's affair ends when girl goes on vacation in July
Jordan Baker moves her ball during golf tournament semifinals
Nick and Jordan attend house-party at Warwick
Jordan leaves borrowed car in rain and lies about it
Nick and Jordan have conversation about careless driving

settings.name: West Egg
settings.description: The less fashionable of two egg-shaped land formations on Long Island, where Nick rents a small house for eighty dollars a month. It's squeezed between enormous mansions and offers a view of the water.

settings.name: East Egg
settings.description: The more fashionable of the two egg-shaped formations, where the white palaces of the wealthy glitter along the water. Home to the Buchanans' elaborate Georgian Colonial mansion.

settings.name: Gatsby's mansion
settings.description: A colossal mansion next to Nick's house, a factual imitation of a Hôtel de Ville in Normandy with a tower, new ivy, marble swimming pool, and more than forty acres of lawn and garden.

settings.name: Buchanan mansion
settings.description: An elaborate cheerful red-and-white Georgian Colonial mansion overlooking the bay, with a quarter-mile lawn running from beach to front door, complete with sundials, brick walks, and burning gardens.

settings.name: Middle Western city
settings.description: Nick's hometown where his family, the Carraways, have been prominent and well-to-do for three generations, and where the family hardware business operates.

settings.name: Valley of ashes
settings.description: A desolate area of land between West Egg and New York where ashes grow like wheat into ridges and hills. Home to ash-grey men who work among the desolate landscape, watched over by the eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg.

settings.name: Wilson's garage
settings.description: George B. Wilson's unprosperous garage in the valley of ashes, part of a small block of yellow brick buildings. Contains a dust-covered Ford and serves as Wilson's business for car repairs and sales.

settings.name: Doctor Eckleburg billboard
settings.description: A giant billboard featuring enormous blue eyes behind yellow spectacles, one yard high retinas, overlooking the valley of ashes. Created by an oculist to advertise his practice.

settings.name: Tom and Myrtle's apartment
settings.description: A small apartment on the top floor at 158th Street in a white apartment building. Crowded with oversized tapestried furniture depicting Versailles scenes, contains living room, dining room, bedroom and bath.

settings.name: Gatsby's library
settings.description: A high Gothic library in Gatsby's mansion, panelled with carved English oak and probably transported complete from some ruin overseas. Contains real books with actual pages.

settings.name: Probity Trust
settings.description: The bond business where Nick works in lower New York, among the white chasms of the financial district.

settings.name: Yale Club
settings.description: A club in New York where Nick takes dinner and studies investments and securities in the library. He finds it the gloomiest event of his day.

settings.name: Warwick
settings.description: Location of a house-party that Nick and Jordan attended together during midsummer.

world_rules: Social class and wealth create distinct geographical and social divisions between East Egg and West Egg
The wealthy elite maintain certain codes of behavior and social expectations
Racial hierarchies and theories about Nordic superiority are accepted topics of conversation among the upper class
Marriage does not preclude extramarital affairs, which are conducted with a degree of social tolerance
The valley of ashes represents the moral and social wasteland between the wealthy areas and New York City
Physical violence is used to maintain control in relationships, particularly by men over women
Social deception and elaborate lies are commonly used to justify personal relationships and desires
Class distinctions affect behavior and personality, with people adopting different personas in different social settings
Gatsby's parties are open to anyone - people attend without formal invitations and often without meeting the host
Wealthy hosts are expected to be extraordinarily generous, replacing damaged items for guests without question
Rumors and speculation about wealthy individuals spread rapidly through party gossip
Professional dishonesty and rule-bending are tolerated if done with sufficient skill and discretion
The wealthy maintain elaborate facades - even Gatsby's books are real rather than fake, showing attention to authentic detail

voice_profile.style_notes: sophisticated vocabulary, long flowing sentences, frequent use of metaphor and symbolism, retrospective narration with philosophical observations

plot_threads.thread: What is the true nature and background of the mysterious Jay Gatsby?
How will Tom's affair with the woman in New York affect his marriage to Daisy?
What is the significance of the green light that Gatsby reaches toward?
Will Nick become romantically involved with Jordan Baker as Daisy suggests?
What was the 'critical, unpleasant story' Nick heard about Jordan Baker?
What caused Nick's disillusionment that temporarily closed out his interest in human nature?
Is Gatsby really related to Kaiser Wilhelm, and where does his wealth actually come from?
Will Tom and Myrtle's relationship continue despite the violence and complications?
How will George Wilson's ignorance of his wife's affair ultimately be resolved?
What role will the valley of ashes and its symbolic wasteland play in the larger story?
What did Gatsby tell Jordan during their private conversation?
What is the truth behind the various rumors about Gatsby (killer, German spy, etc.)?
How will Nick's growing relationship with Jordan develop given her dishonesty?
What is Nick's 'tangle back home' that he needs to resolve?
